brief_document.title: Durable WAL writer with CRC32 checksums and fsync policy
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the durable WAL writer that backs each shard actor's WAL buffer with an append-only file on disk. Mutations are written to disk before acknowledgment. CRC32 checksums enable corruption detection during recovery. Configurable fsync policy lets callers trade latency for durability.
brief_document.task: Implement the durable WAL writer backed by an append-only file. Define the WAL entry format (operation type, key, value, CRC32 checksum). Mutations are written to the durable WAL before the shard actor acknowledges. Implement configurable fsync policy: per-write, every N writes, or commit-only. On commit, truncate the WAL with a truncation marker recording the committed root hash. Truncation must be atomic. Does not implement crash recovery replay (PERSIST-003).

requirements.title: WAL entry format
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a WAL entry format containing: operation type (Put or Delete), key bytes, value bytes (empty for Delete), and a CRC32 checksum of the preceding fields. THE SYSTEM SHALL use a length-prefixed binary format for deterministic serialisation.
acceptance: WalEntry::Put has operation_type, key, value, and crc32 fields
WalEntry::Delete has operation_type, key, and crc32 fields
The CRC32 covers operation_type + key + value
Serialising and deserialising a WalEntry round-trips correctly
WalEntry implements Debug

requirements.title: Append-only WAL file
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL back each shard actor's WAL buffer with an append-only file. WHEN a mutation is appended to the buffer, THE SYSTEM SHALL write the corresponding WalEntry to the file. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT overwrite existing entries — the file is append-only.
acceptance: DurableWal::new(path) opens or creates the WAL file
DurableWal::append(entry) writes the entry to the end of the file
Multiple appends produce sequential entries in the file
The file grows monotonically

requirements.title: Write-before-acknowledge
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL write the WAL entry to the durable file before the shard actor acknowledges the mutation to the caller. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT acknowledge a write until the WAL entry is on disk.
acceptance: A put that crashes after WAL write but before tree commit can be recovered
The shard actor does not return Ok until the WAL entry is written
The WAL file contains the entry even if the process is killed immediately after acknowledgment

requirements.title: Configurable fsync policy
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL accept a fsync policy on construction: PerWrite (fsync after every write), Batched(n) (fsync every n writes), or CommitOnly (fsync only on commit). THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT have a default policy — the caller must choose explicitly.
acceptance: FsyncPolicy::PerWrite fsyncs after every append
FsyncPolicy::Batched(5) fsyncs after every 5th append
FsyncPolicy::CommitOnly fsyncs only when commit is called
Creating a DurableWal without a policy is a compilation error

requirements.title: WAL truncation on commit
requirements.spec: WHEN the WAL buffer is committed (flushed to tree), THE SYSTEM SHALL truncate the WAL file and write a truncation marker containing the committed root hash. THE SYSTEM SHALL make truncation atomic: the marker and root hash are written together so that a crash during truncation does not lose the commit reference.
acceptance: After commit, the WAL file contains only the truncation marker
The truncation marker contains the committed root hash
A crash during truncation leaves either the old WAL (recoverable) or the new marker (committed)
Reading the WAL file after truncation yields zero replay entries and the committed root hash

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement crash recovery replay — that is PERSIST-003
SHALL NOT implement the disk node store — that is PERSIST-001
SHALL NOT implement WAL archival or WAL shipping
SHALL NOT implement a default fsync policy — the caller must choose explicitly
verification: cargo check -p haematite succeeds with no errors
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests
cargo clippy -p haematite produces no errors
grep -r 'unwrap()\|expect(' crates/haematite/src/wal/ --include='*.rs' -l finds no non-test files (excluding buffer.rs from CORE-005)
No file in crates/haematite/src/wal/ exceeds 500 lines

adrs.title: WAL buffer for write amortization rather than direct tree mutation
adrs.decision: WAL buffer over direct tree mutation, because it gives sequential-append write latency (the WAL) with amortized tree cost (one path-to-root rewrite per flush, not per write). The rejected alternative (direct mutation with batching windows) forces callers to accept high write latency or risk high write amplification with small batches.

checklist.text: Each shard actor's WAL buffer is backed by an append-only file on disk.
WAL entries include operation type, key, value (for puts), and a CRC32 checksum (CN2).
Mutations are written to the durable WAL before the shard actor acknowledges the write.
Fsync policy is configurable: per-write, every N writes, or commit-only.
On commit (tree flush), the WAL file is truncated with a truncation marker recording the committed root hash.
WAL truncation is atomic: truncation marker and root hash are written together (CN5).

stories.text: As an application developer, I want to choose the fsync policy (per-write, batched, commit-only) so that I can trade latency for durability based on my workload.
As the Aion engine, I want acknowledged event appends to be durable so that a workflow crash does not lose events the recorder believes are persisted.

constraints.text: Committed tree state must survive unclean shutdown. A committed root hash and all nodes it references must be on disk before commit() returns.
WAL entries must include CRC32 checksums. A corrupted entry must be detectable, not silently replayed.
Shard recovery must be independent. One shard's recovery must not block another shard's startup or write acceptance.
The disk node store directory layout must support at least 10 million nodes without filesystem performance degradation (2-level hash prefix directories).
WAL truncation must be atomic: the truncation marker and committed root hash must be written together so that a crash during truncation does not lose the commit reference.
LRU cache eviction must not affect correctness — a cache miss falls through to disk read.

resolved_context.intention: Persistence is the durability boundary — the line between data that survives a power failure and data that doesn't. When this cluster is complete, haematite writes content-addressed tree nodes to disk with zstd compression, maintains a durable WAL file per shard for crash recovery, and can reconstruct its full state from disk after an unclean shutdown. The experience should be invisible: a developer opens a database, writes to it, and trusts that committed data is durable. No manual recovery steps, no fsck, no WAL management. The content-addressed structure makes this unusually clean — nodes are immutable once written, so there is no in-place update problem, and the WAL is the only source of uncommitted state.

provenance.quote: Persistence is the durability boundary — the line between data that survives a power failure and data that doesn't. When this cluster is complete, haematite writes content-addressed tree nodes to disk
